title: Analogies
subtitle: Word, Letter, Number Analogies & Relationship Patterns

readingSections.title: What is an Analogy?
readingSections.content: An analogy is a comparison between two things that shows how they are similar. Companies use analogy questions to test your reasoning, vocabulary, and pattern recognition skills. These appear in TCS, Infosys, Wipro, Accenture and almost all campus placement exams.

subsections.title: Why Companies Ask Analogy Questions
subsections.content: Companies want people who think logically and spot patterns quickly. Solving analogies proves you can: (1) Understand relationships between concepts, (2) Think abstractly, (3) Make quick decisions under pressure. These are essential IT job skills.

subsections.title: Basic Structure
subsections.content: An analogy looks like: A : B :: C : ? (read as A is to B as C is to what). Your job is to find the relationship between A and B, then apply it to C. Example: Doctor : Hospital :: Teacher : ? Answer: School (a doctor works in a hospital, a teacher works in a school).

subsections.title: Real-Life Example
subsections.content: If your friend says My phone is to me what wings are to a bird, you understand the phone is essential for communication just like wings are essential for flight. You are comparing two relationships that work the same way.

subsections.title: How to Approach
subsections.content: Step 1: Read the first pair carefully. Step 2: Identify the exact relationship. Step 3: Apply the same relationship to C. Step 4: Find the matching answer. Step 5: Double-check by reversing the logic.

readingSections.title: Word Analogies (Most Common)
readingSections.content: Word analogies use regular English words. The relationship can be based on meaning, function, category, or association.

subsections.title: Synonym Analogies (Same Meaning)
subsections.content: The relationship is same meaning. Example: Happy : Joyful :: Sad : ? Answer: Unhappy. Common pairs: Big-Large, Small-Tiny, Fast-Quick, Brave-Courageous, Afraid-Scared.

subsections.title: Antonym Analogies (Opposite Meaning)
subsections.content: The relationship is opposite meaning. Example: Hot : Cold :: Light : ? Answer: Dark. Common pairs: Up-Down, Good-Bad, Happy-Sad, Rich-Poor, Strong-Weak.

subsections.title: Cause and Effect Analogies
subsections.content: One word causes the other. Example: Rain : Flood :: Virus : ? Answer: Disease. Ask yourself: Does A cause B?

subsections.title: Function/Use Analogies
subsections.content: Shows what something is used for. Example: Pen : Write :: Knife : ? Answer: Cut. Pattern: A is used to B.

subsections.title: Part to Whole Analogies
subsections.content: First word is part of the second. Example: Finger : Hand :: Toe : ? Answer: Foot. More: Page : Book, Wheel : Car, Leaf : Tree.

subsections.title: Profession and Tool
subsections.content: Shows what tool a professional uses. Example: Doctor : Stethoscope :: Carpenter : ? Answer: Hammer. Common: Teacher-Chalk, Chef-Knife, Painter-Brush.

subsections.title: Animal and Young Ones
subsections.content: Shows parent and baby relationship. Example: Cat : Kitten :: Dog : ? Answer: Puppy. Memorize: Cow-Calf, Lion-Cub, Sheep-Lamb, Horse-Foal.

subsections.title: Animal and Home/Dwelling
subsections.content: Shows where an animal lives. Example: Dog : Kennel :: Bird : ? Answer: Nest. Learn: Lion-Den, Rabbit-Burrow, Bee-Hive, Horse-Stable.

subsections.title: Country and Capital
subsections.content: Shows capital cities. Example: India : Delhi :: USA : ? Answer: Washington DC. Must-know: India-Delhi, UK-London, France-Paris, Japan-Tokyo.

subsections.title: Person and Achievement
subsections.content: Shows who created something. Example: Shakespeare : Hamlet :: Einstein : ? Answer: Theory of Relativity. Common: Newton-Gravity, Edison-Light Bulb.

readingSections.title: Letter and Number Analogies
readingSections.content: Letter analogies use alphabet patterns and number analogies use mathematical relationships.

subsections.title: Position-Based Letter Analogies
subsections.content: Uses A=1, B=2, C=3...Z=26. Example: A : D :: B : ? Answer: E (A+3=D, B+3=E). Common: +1, +2, +3 shifts, reverse positions, mirror positions.

subsections.title: Skip Pattern Analogies
subsections.content: Letters skip a fixed number. Example: A : C :: E : ? Answer: G (skip 1 letter).

subsections.title: Mirror/Reverse Analogies
subsections.content: A <-> Z, B <-> Y, C <-> X. Example: ABC : ZYX :: DEF : ? Answer: WVU.

subsections.title: Addition/Subtraction Number Patterns
subsections.content: Simple arithmetic. Example: 5 : 8 :: 10 : ? Answer: 13 (add 3).

subsections.title: Multiplication and Square Patterns
subsections.content: Example: 4 : 12 :: 6 : ? Answer: 18 (multiply by 3). Or 3 : 9 :: 5 : ? Answer: 25 (square).

subsections.title: Digit-Based Patterns
subsections.content: Manipulates digits. Example: 12 : 3 :: 45 : ? Answer: 9 (1+2=3, 4+5=9). Or reverse digits.

readingSections.title: Pattern Recognition - Find Relationships Fast
readingSections.content: The key to solving analogies quickly is recognizing the relationship type within 10-15 seconds.

subsections.title: The 5-Second Classification Test
subsections.content: Ask in order: (1) Are they synonyms? (2) Are they opposites? (3) Does one cause the other? (4) Is one part of the other? (5) Is there a functional relationship? Practice until automatic.

subsections.title: Keyword Triggers
subsections.content: Similar meanings -> Synonym. Opposite meanings -> Antonym. Action words -> Function. Place names -> Location. Person names -> Achievement. Numbers -> Mathematical. Animals -> Young One or Home.

subsections.title: Sentence Frame Technique
subsections.content: Force into a sentence: A is a type of B, A uses B, A lives in B, A causes B, A is opposite of B. The frame that fits tells you the relationship.

subsections.title: Elimination Strategy
subsections.content: When stuck: (1) Cross out options that don't match word type. (2) Eliminate reversed relationships. (3) Remove overly specific or general answers. Usually 2-3 options get eliminated quickly.

subsections.title: Common Traps
subsections.content: Trap 1: Reversed relationship. Trap 2: Partial match. Trap 3: Association without true relationship. Trap 4: Too specific. Always verify: Does C relate to my answer exactly like A relates to B?

readingSections.title: Company-Specific Patterns
readingSections.content: Different companies favor different analogy types. Knowing their patterns gives you an edge.

subsections.title: TCS Patterns
subsections.content: TCS heavily tests: Profession-Tool, Animal-Young, Country-Capital, Synonym-Antonym mix. Difficulty: Medium. Time: 45 seconds per question.

subsections.title: Infosys Patterns
subsections.content: Infosys prefers: Cause-Effect, Part-Whole, Letter analogies with complex skips, Function-based. Difficulty: Medium-Hard.

subsections.title: Wipro Patterns
subsections.content: Wipro focuses on: Synonym-Antonym, Number analogies with squares/cubes, Simple function. Difficulty: Easy-Medium.

subsections.title: Accenture/Capgemini
subsections.content: Accenture: Mix of word and letter analogies, emphasis on speed. Capgemini: Loves classification analogies.

subsections.title: Cognizant/HCL
subsections.content: Cognizant: Straightforward word analogies, synonyms/antonyms. HCL: More abstract relationships and GK-based analogies.

readingSections.title: Speed Shortcuts and Memory Tricks
readingSections.content: Master these to solve analogies in 20-30 seconds.

subsections.title: The 3-Second Relationship Label
subsections.content: The moment you read A:B, mentally say: Opposites!, Same!, Tool!, Home!, Baby!, Capital!. This labels the relationship instantly and prevents overthinking.

subsections.title: EJOTY Trick for Letter Positions
subsections.content: Remember E=5, J=10, O=15, T=20, Y=25. Use these anchors to find positions quickly. M is 2 before O(15) so M=13.

subsections.title: Memory Tricks for Capitals
subsections.content: Create silly sentences: Delhi is India capital, DC is USA capital, London has Queen, Paris has Eiffel Tower. Weird associations are memorable.

subsections.title: Reverse Check Shortcut
subsections.content: After picking your answer, quickly verify by reversing: If A:B::C:D works, then B:A::D:C should also make sense. If not, reconsider!

subsections.title: Animal Young Grouping
subsections.content: Group by pattern: Cub endings for big cats/bears, Calf for large animals (cow, elephant, whale), Lamb for sheep, Foal for horses, Chick for birds.

formulas.title: Synonym Relationship Rule
formulas.explanation: When A and B have similar meanings, find a word with similar meaning to C.

formulas.title: Antonym Relationship Rule
formulas.formula: A = opposite of B => C = opposite of ?
formulas.explanation: When A and B are opposites, find the opposite of C.

formulas.title: Cause and Effect Rule
formulas.explanation: When A causes B, find what C causes.

formulas.title: Function/Use Rule
formulas.formula: A is used to B => C is used to ?
formulas.explanation: When B is the function of A, find the function of C.

formulas.title: Part to Whole Rule
formulas.formula: A is part of B => C is part of ?
formulas.explanation: When A is part of B, find what C is part of.

formulas.title: Profession and Tool Rule
formulas.explanation: When B is the tool of profession A, find the tool of profession C.

formulas.title: Animal and Young One Rule
formulas.explanation: When B is the baby of animal A, find the baby of animal C.

formulas.title: Animal and Dwelling Rule
formulas.formula: A lives in B => C lives in ?
formulas.explanation: When B is the home of animal A, find the home of animal C.

formulas.title: Country and Capital Rule
formulas.explanation: When B is capital of country A, find capital of country C.

formulas.title: Letter Shift Formula
formulas.explanation: When B is n positions after A in alphabet, find n positions after C.

formulas.title: Number Square Rule
formulas.explanation: When second number is square of first.

formulas.title: Digit Sum Rule
formulas.explanation: Sum the digits of the number.
